13. OTHER BUSINESS   
Councillor Gu – Announcement  
Councillor Gu advised that she will not be seek ing re -election. The following were 
Councillor Gu’s remarks:  
“I was elected at the age of 24 by Burnaby residents with a vision to make Burnaby a 
fairer, compassionate, affordable, and sustainable city. Together with the BCA team, it 
has been a privilege t o work in service of these goals. Some of the work I am most 
proud of includes the full decarbonization of all new housing, the protection of Cariboo 
Heights Forest in full and in perpetuity, helping get a City -owned, non -profit, and nimble 
affordable hous ing developer (the Burnaby Housing Authority!) off the ground and start 
its first two projects, working to help build out a district energy system that will retrofit 
the city's biggest emitters, initiating a climate hazards mapping process, getting a bike 
learning zone (bike playground) started, and winning back public taxpayer funds from 
large polluters.  
I've also had the opportunity to make policy change: strengthening language to better 
protect tenants, securing more transparency and affordable housing, using density 
bonus policy to target the creation of affordable housing and childcare, amending 
bylaws to protect more green space, trees, and agricultural land, improving safety for 
pedestrians in construction zones, improving cycling infrastructure throu ghout the city, 
improving public transit options throughout the city, adding an equity lens to our 
transportation demand management strategy, and more.  18
Open Council Meeting  
Minutes March 24 , 2026  
 
 13 It has been a privilege to advocate on behalf of Burnaby residents to the provincial and 
federal governm ents on a 2 -way arms embargo and reunification of Palestinian families, 
on better tools to protect urban waterways, for systemic changes to improve traffic 
safety, securing investments in Burnaby child care projects, and so many more issues.  
After careful thought, I have decided that I won't be seeking re -election at the end of this 
term. The demands of this role are significant, and I believe it's important to recognize 
when it's time to recharge and refocus. While I will always be fighting for the public 
good, a fairer and more just world, and a better Burnaby, it won't be as a City Councillor 
after October.  
I want to thank my Council colleagues. We have had our fair share of disagreements, 
tonight being an example, but overwhelmingly we are lucky in Burna by to have had a 
collaborative and well -functioning Council the last 4 years. I also want to thank staff, 
whom I've had the chance to get to know well through many deep -dive conversations on 
policy and issues, and whom I have often pushed hard on how we ca n squeeze every 
ounce of public benefit out of every plan, policy, and decision. As always, 
implementation is the most critical piece of any council decision that is made, and I look 
forward to watching the successful outcomes of some of the planning proce sses I've 
had the chance to be a part of - I'll take another opportunity here to mention again that I 
expect full and successful implementation of our recently adopted urban forestry 
strategy.   
It has been an honour beyond words to have been able to work o n countless issues that 
are profoundly important to me and that which shape the lives of so many. Every 
decision I have made has been guided by what I believe to be right, and that has always 
been defined by a belief in the responsibility we have to each o ther and this earth in our 
short lives.  
So much of the world is in crisis, with what feels like ever -increasing instability and ever -
worsening and overlapping crises. There is so much suffering, corporations that profit 
off this suffering, and those who we ave political narratives around and at expense of 
this suffering for their own benefit.  
Now, more than ever, I believe we need to see elected officials govern with competence 
and compassion, who unite rather than divide, and who operate with integrity. Thank 
you, Burnaby, for entrusting me with the responsibility of our city and our future. It was 
never taken lightly.”  
Members of Council formally acknowledged Councillor Gu’s dedication to the City of 
Burnaby and noted that her absence will be felt by all .

3. INVITED PRESENTATION  
3.1 Chris Bowcock - Burnaby Citizens Exposures to Chlorine Release Incidents  
 Presenters:  Chris Bowcock, Retired Fire Chief, and Michael Graveson, 
Consultant, Chlorine Industry  
 
Chris Bowcock, Retired Fire Chief, City of Burnaby, and Michael Graveson, 
Consultant, Chlorine Industry, appeared before Council providing information on 
significant safety risks associated with chlorine release incidents on Burnaby 
residents.  
Chris Bowcock provided an introduction to the Chemtrade Plant located in the 
District of North Vancouver (DNV) and noted that the facility is manufacturing 
liquid chlor ine. The speaker noted that, under the existing DNV bylaw, the 
production of liquid chlorine would need to cease by 2030, and highlighted that a 
bylaw amendment application to allow chlorine production received first reading 
on January 12, 2026, and two Pu blic Hearings, on February 24 and 25, 2026. 
Public input has now concluded, with the second the third reading scheduled for 
April 13, 2026.  
C. Bowcock continued his presentation by outlining the risks that production and 
transportation of chlorine pose to Burnaby residents, as an average of four liquid 
chlorine rail cars travel through Burnaby on a daily basis. In the event of chlorine 
gas release from one or more of the rail cars, Burnaby residents would face 
significant fatality risk from toxic inhalation , potentially leading to respiratory 
distress, immediate and irreparable damage to lungs, as well as chemical burns 
to respiratory tissues. The speaker indicated that the DNV bylaw amendment has 
not taken into consideration the risk associated with the chl orine rail car release 
outside of the Chemtrade facility.  
*Councillor Keithley left at 5:06 and returned at 5:23 pm  
*Councillor Dhaliwal joined at 5:15 pm

magnitude of emergency response that would be required within Burnaby in the 22

 3 event of chlorine gas release. If no personal protected equipment would be

The speaker outlined that the risk assessment supporting the proposed bylaw 
amendment, done by AcuTech, did not  consider rail car movements of liquid 
chlorine beyond the Chemtrade facility property line. As a result of this omission, 
Chris Bowcock expressed concerns regarding the impact to Burnaby residents in 
an event of chlorine gas leak. Chris Bowcock concluded his presentation by 
asserting that given this potential impact, the City of Burnaby should be 
considered a critical stakeholder in this issue.

Michael Graveson provided a PowerPoint presentation noting that Canadian 
water is not treated directly with chlorine, it is treated with bleach, and indicated 
that less than 5% of chlorine from Chemtrade facility is directed towards water 
treatm ent in Western Canada. The speaker echoed Chris Bowcock's concerns 
regarding chlorine transportation being the biggest risk.  
The speaker highlighted that bleach is used for treating water in Canada, and 
there are logistics in place for transporting bleach . While chlorine is used to make 
bleach, over 90% of chlorine produced by Chemtrade is exported to the United 
States.  
*Councillor Dhaliwal left at 6:13 pm and returned at 6:15 pm  
*Councillor Calendino left at 6:15 pm and did not return  
 
M. Graveson provided an overview of chlorine use in Canada, including organic 
chemicals and plastics (40 -50%), inorganic chemicals (20 -25%), chlorinated 
intermediates (10 -15%), pulp and paper (8 -10%), water treatment (5 -8%), 
pharmaceuticals and agriculture (3 -5%), as well as other uses (5%).  
The speaker stated that Chemtrade uses potentially misleading language by 
asserting that chlorine is used to treat drinking water in Canada, noting that 
bleach is typically used for this purpose instead. Michel Graveson further not ed 
that information published on the Chemtrade website indicates that the majority 
of the chlorine produced is exported to the United States.  
The speaker indicated that the website does not adequately describe the storage 
and shipping model for chlorine, a s it focuses on shipping logistics but does not 
address the storage of rail cars containing chlorine outside the facility building 23

SUBJECT:  CERTIFICATE OF SUFFICIENCY : NORTH ROAD  BIA RENEWAL  
 
PURPOSE : To provide results for the petitions submitted against the proposed North 
Road  Business Improvement Area (BIA) renewal bylaw for a 5-year 
period . 
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the report titled “ Certificate of Sufficiency : North Road  BIA Renewal” dated 
April 14, 2026  be received for information ; 
THAT the Certificate of Sufficiency  dated April 14, 2026, certified by the Corporate 
Officer and included as Attachment 1 to this report, be received; and  
THAT a copy of this report be sent to the President of the  Burnaby North Road 
Business Improvement Association . 
 
1.0 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY  
On February 2 4, 202 6, Council authorized staff to work with the Burnaby North Road 
Business Improvement Association (the “Association”) towards the renewal of  the 
Burnaby North Road  Business Improvement Area ( BNR BIA) for a period of five years, 
from Apri l 1, 2026 to March 31, 2031 . This report provide s Council with the results of the 
Council Initiative petition  process re lated  to the proposed renewal of the BNRBIA  and 
advis es Council not to  proceed with adoption of the proposed BNRBIA renewal bylaw.  
2.0  POLICY SECTION  
 
Under the Community Charter , the City can use one of two mechanisms to confirm 
support or objection to a business improvement area: 
• Section 212 - Petition “For” a Local Area Service  
• Section 213 - Local Area Service on Council Initiative — Subject to Petition 
“Against” a Local Area Service  
The process commonly followed by municipalities across BC for business improvement 
areas  and the one that the City previously followed is the “Local Area Service on Council 
Initiative — Petition “Against” process. For the “Petition Against” process, the City mail ed 
out notices to owners of all Class 5 (Light industrial) and Class 6 (Business) properties 
within the proposed BNR BIA boundary seeking to hear from those who are opposed to 31

renewal of the BNR BIA within a 30-day petition period. Council may not proceed with the 
BNR BIA if t hose signing as not in support of the BNR BIA represent at least 50% of the 
parcels in the BNR BIA and at least 50% of the assessed value of land and improvements 
in the BNR BIA. 
3.0 BACKGROUND  
On February 2 3, 202 6, Council considered  and gave three readings  to the Burnaby 
Business Improvement Area ( Burnaby North Road ) Bylaw 202 6, Bylaw No. 14 797.  
The Bylaw define d the boundaries of the BIA and estimated  the total local service tax that 
will be charged based on the current assessed value of land or improvements , or both, 
that fall within Class 5 (Light Industry) or Class 6 (Business and Other) properties within 
the BNR BIA boundary.   
Before the bylaw can be adopted, staff are required to undertake a 30-day petition 
process to determine opposition for the BNR BIA from all Class 5 and 6 property owners 
within the BNR BIA’s proposed boundaries. Notices and petition l etters  were sent to the 
property owners . Tenants of the subject properties were also sent notification letters. 
Owner notices and tenant letters included  the estimated charges for the Local Area 
Service Tax  based on completed assessment values , the boundaries of the BNR BIA, the 
petition submission deadline, as well as instructions  on how to submit petitions and tenant 
opposition letters to the City.  
4.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
Under the Community Charter , the City can use one of two mechanisms to confirm 
support  or objection to a business improvement area :  
• Section 212 - Petition “ For” a Local Area Service  
• Section 213 - Local Area Service on Council Initiative — Petition “Against” a Local     
Area Service       
The process followed by all municipalities across BC for business improvement 
associations  is the “Local Area Service on Council Initiative — Petition Against”  under  
Section 213 of the Community Charter , despite the proposal for a BIA typically being 
initiated by the businesses within the BIA  or the BIA s. 
For “petition s against”  to be deemed sufficient , the petitions against  the business 
improvement area  must be signed by the owners of at least 50% of the parcels that would 
be subject to the local area tax for the proposed project, and the petitioners must be the 
owners of parcels that in total represent at least 50% of the assessed value of land and 
impro vements that would be subject to the local service tax.   
The BNR BIA petition process opened on  February 2 5, 202 6, and closed on 
March  30, 2026, at 4:45 p m. During this time, staff under the direction of the Corporate 
Officer collect ed opposition petition  letters received  from the  applicable  property owners .   32

4.3 Next steps  
Under subsections 213(6) and (7) of the Community Charter, if a council has been 
prevented from undertaking a local area service because of a sufficient petition 
against, the council must not propose the same service on its own initiative within a 
period of one year after the presentation of the petition unless th e proposed local area 
service is varied from or less expensive than that originally proposed. Should the 
Association  wish to make a nother  attempt at renewing the BNRBIA under Section  213 
of the Community Charter (local area service on C ouncil initiative — subject to petition 
against ) within the next year , then the service must be varied from or less expensive 
than that originally proposed . The timing required ( 30-day petition process, plus 
required bylaw readings) for this process would mean that it could not be completed in 
time for the 2026 tax levy  (mid-May) .  
Staff would therefore recommend t hat if the Association makes another request to 
renew the BNRBIA, that  Council not consider initiat ing another local area service on 
Council initiative for the BNRBIA  until the fall, provided that the Association can 
demonstrate that there is sufficient owner support for the BNRBIA . Alternatively, the 
Association may, under Section 212 of the Community Charter , petition for a local a rea 
service. In order for a petition for a local area service under Section 212 of the 
Community Charter to be certified as sufficient and valid, the petition must be signed 
by the owners of at least 50% of the parcels that would be subject to the local service 
tax, and the persons signing must be the owners of parcels that in total represent at 
least 50% of the assessed value of land and improvements that would be subject to 
the local service tax.  
5.0 COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
Public notice was provided on the City’s Public Notice page as well as the City’s 
eNewsletter , in accordance with the City’s Public Notice Bylaw.  
Received owner and tenant opposition letters were  available for public inspection in the 
Legislative Services department at City Hall for two weeks from March 30, 2026, until 
April 1 4, 202 6. 
6.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Given that sufficient petitions in opposition to the renewal of the BNR BIA were received  
which prevents Council from adopting the Burnaby Business Improvement Area (Burnaby 
North Road) Bylaw, 2026 , mill rates for the BRNBIA will not be included within the 
Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 , and the City will not undertake  billing and collection of funds 
in 2026 for the BNRBIA . As a result, the Association will not recei ve grant funding for the 
period from April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2027 .

3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
3.1 Shareholder Resolutions in Lieu of 2026 AGM  
Under section 182(1) of the Business Corporations  Act (BCA), a company is 
required to hold an  AGM at least once in each calendar year and not more than 15 
months after the annual reference date for the preceding calendar year, unless  the 
shareholders  unanimously resolve to waive it or unanimously pass written 
resolutions dealing with all business normally conducted at the AGM.  
When written resolutions are passed in place of holding an AGM , the shareholder 
must select an annual reference date – representing the date the AGM would 
otherwise have taken place – to maintain statutory continuity. The City, as sole 
shareholder of the BHA, may pass such resolutions to transact the business of the 
AGM in lieu of holding an AGM.  
3.2 Annual  Reference  Date  
Under the BCA, the  Annual Reference Date (“ARD”)  is defined  as the date, 
selected by shareholders, that the  Annual General Meeting is held.  If the 
shareholders, by an unanimous resolution, consent to all of the business required 
to be transacted at an AGM, then then the shareholders must select as  the 
company’s  ARD  a date that would  be appropriate  for the holding  of the AGM  that 
is being waived. The BHA  has requested that the City set December 12, 2026 as 
the annual reference date.  
 
3.3 Appointment of Directors  
Under s.13.1 of the BHA’s Articles, at every AGM, the City, as sole shareholder, will 
appoint a board of directors for the BHA.  Under the BHA Articles, the BHA Board 
consists of 10 directors, four of which are affiliated with the City (“City Directors”) 
and six of which are independent of the City and individuals with experience and 
expertise in various aspects of housing and real estate development (“Independent 
Directors”).   
To ensure the entire Board does not turnover at the same time, the BHA Articles 
stagger the initial terms of the directors with five Independent Directors serving an 
initial two -year term and the four City Directors and one Independent Director 
serving an initial three -year term. At every succeeding AGM, those directors whose 
term on the Board is set to expire will be re -appointed or replaced for subsequent 
three -year terms. The Articles limit any director from serving more than two 
consecutive terms or a m aximum of six consecutive years to ensure a diversity of 
perspectives on the BHA Board. The Articles permit past directors to be eligible for 
reappointment after an absence of at least one year.  
The current board of directors was appointed by the City, as sole shareholder, on 
an interim basis until the BHA’s first AGM.  As the City waived the 2025 AGM, the 
interim directors continued as directors . As part of the business to be conducted at 
the 2026 AGM, the City, as sole shareholder, must appoint directors to the BHA 
Board.  It is recommended that Council appoint the current interim directors as 
directors to the BHA Board for either a two - or three -year term as indicated below:

2.0 BACKGROUND  
The Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) is a wholly owned subsidiary of the 
City of Burnaby, incorporated on June 14, 2024, as a local government corporation under 
the BC Business Corporations Act . The BHA was established to support the City’s 
strategic objective of increasing the supply of non -market and market housing. Its 
mandate is to invest in, develop, acquire, hold, and manage land and buildings to deliver 
housing across a range of affordabi lity levels. The BHA prepares its financial statemen ts 
in accordance with Canadian Public Sector Accounting Standards (PSAS).  
 
In accordance with the  Community Charter , the City and the BHA must enter into a 
partnering agreement before the City can provide assistance. In 2025, the City and the 
BHA executed two such agreements, defining the assistance to be provided and the 
housing -related services to be delivered in ret urn. On February 11, 2025, Council 
approved the first partnering agreement  (PA No. 1),  which provides $2,000,000 in annual 
operating funding for a five -year period.  Release of Year 2 operating funding is contingent 
on Council’s receipt and approval of the BHA’s Final Strategic Plan and receipt of the 
BHA’s 2025 Financial Statements and updated 2026 Operating Budget.  
Council approved the BHA’s 2026 -2030 Final Strategic Plan  at its open meeting on March 
24, 2026.  
The purpose of this report is to present the BHA’s  2025 Annual Financial Statements for 
the period from June 14, 2024 to fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, being its first full 
year of operations, and its 2026 Operating Budget to City Council.  
The BHA’s 2025 Annual Financial Statements ( Attachment 1 ) were approved by the BHA 
Board on February 26, 2026. The BHA’s 2026 Operating Budget ( Attachment 2 ) was 
approved by the BHA Board on November 18, 2025.  
Representatives from the BHA will attend the Council Meeting to respond to Council’s 
questions about the 2025 Financial Statements and the updated 2026 Operating Budget.

Authority’s inaugural financial statements for the period June 14, 2024 to December 
31, 2025.  The auditor confirmed that the statements fairly present the BHA’s financial 
posit ion, results of operations, changes in net financial assets, and cash flows in 
accordance with Canadian Public Sector Accounting Standards. This unqualified 
conclusion demonstrates that the BHA’s first reporting period was presented in a 
transparent, accur ate, and compliant manner, with no matters requiring modification 
of the auditor’s opinion.

3.2  Financial Reporting  
Accounting Framework  
The BHA prepares its financial statements in accordance with Canadian Public Sector 
Accounting Standards (PSAS) and follows full accrual accounting. Statements were 
prepared on a going concern basis, supported by ongoing financial contributions from 
the Ci ty. 
Overall Financial Summary  
As of December 31, 2025, the BHA reported an accumulated surplus of $7.52 million, 
made up of $5.55 million in tangible capital assets and $1.9 7 million in operating 
surplus . Cash holdings total led $31.75 million, including a $25 million GIC maturing in 
November 2026 .  The BHA reported receivables of $326,073 and payables of 
$619,696.  
From incorporation to year -end, the BHA generated $8.21 million in revenue, primarily 
from City grants, and incurred $683,684 in expenses, resulting in a $7.52 million 
annual surplus.    For its initial year of operations, the BHA incurred expenses equal to 
roughly 28% of its total operating budget, because core staff positions (e.g. CEO, 
Financial Controller) .  Staff  were still being hired,  and operational activities were just 
starting up.  
Net financial assets increased by $1.95 million, reflecting the annual surplus offset by 
$5.55 million in capital asset acquisitions and minor changes in non -financial assets.  
Cash flows were strong, with $37.3 0 million provided by operating activities, mainly 
due to  an annual surplus of $7.52 million and  an increase of $29.5 0 million in deferred 
revenue, and $5.55 million used for capital purchases, yielding a net cash increase of 
$31.75 million from zero at incorporation.  
City Funding and Related Party Transactions  
Between incorporation and the 2025 year-end, the BHA received $37.48 million in 
grants from the City, which remain its primary funding source. These include startup, 
operating, and capital grants intended to support establishment costs, annual 
operating requirements, and project development acti vities.  
The City also provided administrative services during initial setup, incurred prior to the 
BHA hiring its current employees, and the BHA made payments for development 
related fees. The BHA remains economically dependent on the City for its ongoing 
operatio nal and capital needs.  
Deferred Revenue and Project Funding  
Deferred revenue totaled $29.51 million at yearend, representing unspent restricted 
contributions allocated to two major development projects. Revenue is recognized as 
project related conditions and performance obligations are met.   Project specific 
allocations  include:  
• 10th Avenue & Newcombe Street Project: $23.21 million  
• Marine Drive (Edmonds) Project: $6.30 million  
These amounts reflect the portion of City funding not yet recognized as revenue.  43

Capital Projects and Asset Development  
Tangible capital assets amounted to $5.55 million, primarily representing assets under 
development for the BHA’s two major housing initiatives. Assets under development 
are capitalized until ready for use, and no impairments or asset retirement obligations 
were identified.  
The City’s major housing developments include the 10th Avenue & Newcombe Street 
project, featuring two six -storey rental buildings with 204 units offering a mix of 
affordable and market homes with shared amenities, and the Marine Drive (Edmonds) 
developmen t, which will provide 183 non-market and market rental townhomes in two - 
and three -bedroom layouts, complemented by outdoor amenity spaces.  
Financial Resilience and Risk Management  
The BHA holds multi -year contractual obligations for services and benefits from 
Partnering Agreements with the City, which provide ongoing operating and capital 
funding subject to performance criteria.  
The BHA’s exposure to financial risk is assessed as low. Conservative investment 
practices minimize credit and market risk, while liquidity risk is mitigated through 
regular cash flow monitoring and secure funding commitments from the City.  
Operating Highlights  
In its inaugural year, the BHA achiev ed several significant milestones. The BHA 
executed partnering agreements with the City of Burnaby, securing $475,000 in initial 
startup  funding, annual operating funding of $2.0 0 million, and authorization for up to 
$100 .00 million in financing to support the delivery of new housing.  
To establish a strong foundation for future operations , the BHA hired a core leadership 
and project team, including its Chief Executive Officer, Financial Controller, Project 
Coordinator & Operations Manager, and Development Manager.  
The BHA also advanced its first two major housing initiatives:  
• 10th Avenue & Newcombe Street Project  – This development will deliver two 
six storey rental buildings comprising 204 units, offering a mix of affordable and 
market homes with shared amenity spaces.  
• Marine Drive (Edmonds) Development  – This project will provide 183 
affordable and market rental townhomes in two - and three -bedroom 
configurations, complemented by outdoor amenity areas designed to support 
family -oriented living.

Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (the Corporation), a wholly owned subsidiary of the City of 
Burnaby (the City), wa s incorporated under the Business Corporations Act  (British Columbia) as a

1. Significant accounting policies: 
The Corporations financial statements are prepar ed in accordance with Canadian public sector 
accounting standards (PSAS) as issu ed by the Public Sector Accounting Board of the Chartered 
Professional Accountants of Canada. Significant accounting policies adopted by the Corporation 
are as follows: 
(a) Basis of accounting: 
The Corporation follows the accrual method of accounting for revenues and expenses.  
Revenues are recognized based on revenue recognition policy (Note 1(c)). Expenses are 
recognized as they are incurred and measurable as a result of receipt of goods or services 
and/or the legal obligation to pay.  
These financial statements have been prepared on the basis that the Corporation is a going 
concern, which assumes that the Corporation will continue to realize its assets and discharge 
its liabilities in the normal course of operations. The Corporat ions ability to continue as a 
going concern is dependent upon, among other things, the continued financial support of the 
City. The Citys operating and capital budgets incorporate sufficient financial support for the 
Corporation to continue its operations. 
(b) Government transfers: 
Government transfers are recognized as revenue in the financial statements when the 
transfer is authorized and any eligibility criteria are met, except to the extent that transfer 
stipulations give rise to an obligation that meets the definition of a liability. The transfer of 
revenue is initially deferred and then recognized in the Statement of Operations and 
Accumulated Surplus as the stipulations are met and the liability is settled. 
When the Corporation is deemed the transferor, the transfer expense is recognized when the 
recipient is authorized and has met the eligibility criteria. 
(c) Revenue: 
Revenues from transactions with performance obligations are recognized when (at a point in 
time) or as (over a period of time), the Corporation satisfies the performance obligations, 
which occur when control of the benefits associated with the promised goods or services has 
passed to the payor.  
Revenues from transactions without performance obligations are recognized at realizable 
value when the Corporation has the right to claim or retain an inflow of economic resources 
received or receivable and there is a past transaction or event that gives rise to the economic 
resources.  56

(d) Deferred revenue: 
Deferred revenue represents restricted contributions, revenues, and/or grants which have 
been collected, but which the related services or obligations have yet to be performed. These 
amounts will be recognized as revenue in the fiscal year the Corporation satisfies the 
performance obligations, services are rendered, and/or when qualifying expenditures are 
incurred. 
(e) Interest income: 
Interest income is recorded as revenue in the period earned. When required, based on 
external requirements, interest income earned on deferred revenue is added to and forms 
part of the deferred revenue. 
(f) Cash and cash equivalents: 
Cash and cash equivalent include cash and short-term investments with maturities of three 
months or less at the date of acquisition, are readily convertible to known amounts of cash 
and are subject to an insignificant risk of change in value. 
(g) Non-financial assets: 
Non-financial assets are not available to discharge existing liabilities and are held for use in 
the provision of services. They may have useful lives extending beyond the current year and 
are not intended for sale in the ordinary course of operations. 
(i) Tangible capital assets: 
Tangible capital assets are initially recorded at cost which includes amounts that are 
directly attributable to acquisition, construction, development or betterment of the asset, 
including asset retirement costs that are capitalized as part of the carrying amount of the 
related tangible capital asset. The cost, less residual value, of the tangible capital assets, 
except land, is amortized over the estimated useful lives, commencing the month and 
year the asset is put into service.

useful life. Assets under development are transferred to the appropriate asset class and 
are amortized from the date the asset is available for use. 57

11. Financial risk management: 
The Corporation has exposure to the following financial risks from its use of financial instruments: 
credit risk, market risk, interest rate risk and liquidity risk. Management is responsible for 
safeguarding resources, managing risks, and implementing appropriate policies and framework. 
This note presents information on how the Corporation manages those financial risks. 
(a) Credit risk: 
Credit risk refers to the potential for the Corporation to incur financial losses if a third party 
fails to fulfill its contractual obligations. Such risks arise principally from certain financial 
assets held by the Corporation consisting cash and cash equivalents, and accounts 
receivables. The Corporation limits its credit risk by holding its cash and cash equivalent with 
federally regulated, credit worthy institutions. The Corporation assesses these financial 
assets on a continuous basis for any amounts that are not collectible or realizable. 
It is managements opinion that the Corporation is  not exposed to significant credit risk from 
financial instruments. 
(b) Market risk and interest rate risk: 
Market risk is the risk that changes in market prices, such as interest rates, will affect the 
Corporations income. The objective of market risk management is to control market risk 
exposures within acceptable parameters while optimizing the return of risk. 
Interest rate risk is the risk that the fair value of future cash flows of a financial instrument will 
fluctuate because of changes in the market interest rate. 
It is managements opinion that the Corporati on is not exposed to significant market or 
interest rate risk from its financial instruments. 
(c) Liquidity risk: 
Liquidity risk is the risk that the Corporation will not be able to meet its financial obligations as 
they come due. The Corporation manages liquidity risks by continually monitoring actual and 
forecasted cash flows from operations and anticipated investing and financing activities to 
ensure, as far as possible, that it will always have sufficient liquidity to meet its liabilities when 
due, under both normal and stressed conditions, without incurring unacceptable losses or 
risking damage to the Corporations reputation. 
It is managements opinion that the Corporation is not exposed to significant liquidity risk.

SUBJECT:  PROPOSED BYLAW AMENDMENTS TO ADDRESS E-BIKE SHARE 
PILOT PROGRAM AND PROVINCIAL ELECTRIC KICK SCOOTER 
PILOT PROGRAM  
 
PURPOSE : To propose amendments to the Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw and the 
Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw to  implement  the E -Bike Share Pilot 
Program and address the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program  
within the City . 
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward amendments to  the 
following bylaws to implement the E -Bike Share Pilot Program and address the 
provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program:  
(a) Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961 ; 
(b) Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 ; and  
(c) Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 , 
as described in the report titled “Proposed Bylaw Amendments to Address  E-Bike 
Share Pilot Program and Provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program” dated 
April 14 , 2026.  
1.0 POLICY SECTION  
This report responds to the City of Burnaby’s Corporate Strategic Plan and goals for a 
connected and dynamic community and is aligned with our Council -adopted policies, 
plans, and strategies within the Burnaby Transportation Plan, Connecting Burnaby . 
2.0 BACKGROUND  
The City of Burnaby is preparing to launch an E -Bike Share Pilot Program as part of 
ongoing efforts to expand sustainable transportation options, reduce dependence on 
personal  vehicles, and advance municipal transportation goals. The E -Bike Share Pilot 
Program will introduce a convenient, low -emission option for short distance trips, 
helping residents travel with ease without relying on personal vehicles . 
This initiative builds on the City’s participation in the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot 
Program, which came into effect on January 1, 2026, allowing residents to operate 
privately owned electric kick scooters in Burnaby . Together, these programs diversify 65
Subject: Proposed Bylaw Amendments to Address  E-Bike Share Pilot Program and Provincial Electric 
Kick Scooter Pilot Program  
Open Council Meeting  April 14, 2026  ................................ ...........  Page 2 
 
transportation options, promote healthier and more active travel, and reduce traffic 
congestion and greenhouse gas emissions.  
Integrating both e -bikes and e -scooters into Burnaby’s mobility network  will provide 
residents with more flexible, affordable, and environmentally friendly ways to travel 
throughout  the community.  
Enforc ement of  the operation and us e of personal and shared e -bikes and electric kick 
scooters will primarily fall under the jurisdiction of  law enforcement (i.e. the Burnaby 
RCMP) , as these are considered moving vehicle violations and infractions are regulated  
under the Motor Vehicle Act (MVA).  
This report presents several amendments to the Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw , 
Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw and Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw  to 
address the following on City streets and parks:  
• the Burnaby E -Bike Share Pilot Program; and  
• the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program.  
3.0 PROPOSED BYLAW AMENDMENTS  
In order to implement the proposed E-Bike Share Pilot Program and address the 
provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program , amendments to the following bylaws are 
required:  
• Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961  
• Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979  
• Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009  
The proposed amendments to these bylaws are set out in Attachment 1  of this report  
and key changes are described below:  
Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw  
• Introduce key definitions such as Cycles, Motor Assisted Cycles, Electric Kick 
Scooters, Shared Electric -Bikes, and Shared Micromobility Devices, and to 
establish rules for their use in the public right -of-way. 
• Authorize the City to designate Shared Micromobility Parking Zones, regulate 
fleet deployment, parking, and device speed, and clarify operator 
responsibilities.  
Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw  
• Introduce key definitions for Cycles, Motor Assisted Cycles, and Electric Kick 
Scooters . 66

• Amend to provide that no person may use a Cycle, Motor Assisted Cycle, or 
Electric Kick Scooter within a park except in areas specifically designated for 
such use.  
Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw  
• Amend violation provision for Section 44 of the Parks Regulation Bylaw to 
add driving or riding a Cycle, Motor Assisted Cycle or Electric Kick Scooter in 
an area not designated for such use. As explained above, violations of e-
bikes and electric kick scooters are considered moving vehicle offences and 
enforced by the RCMP under the Motor Vehicle Act (MVA).  
4.0 COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
Staff will develop targeted communication and public education materials to support the 
launch of the E -Bike Share Pilot Program and the City’s continued participation in the 
provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program. The focus will be on encouraging the 
safe and lawful use of e -bikes and privately owned electric kick scooters, and on raising 
awareness of new bylaw requirements and designated riding and parking areas. 
Communications may include updates to the City’s website, social media content, and 
printed materials . 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The E -Bike Share Pilot Program will generate revenue from the number of devices 
operating in the City and the usage of the device s. The new revenue will contribute to 
the E -Bike Share Pilot Program ’s operational and maintenance costs for the City.

COUNCIL REPORT  
 
TO: MAYOR & COUNCIL LORS  
 
FROM:  GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT   
SUBJECT:  PROVINCIAL HOUSING TARGET ORDER 6 -MONTH INTERIM 
PROGRESS REPORT  
 
PURPOSE : To present the Housing Target Order and obtain Council authorization to 
forward Burnaby’s  Housing Target Progress R eport Form to the BC 
Ministry of Housing and Municipal Affairs, as required by the Housing 
Supply Act  and Housing Supply Regulation . 
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the report titled “Provincial Housing Target Order 6 -Month Interim Progress 
Report ”, dated April 14, 2026, be received for information ; and 
THAT  the General Manager Planning and Development  be authorized  to submit 
the Housing Target Progress Report Form , as found in Attachment 2 , to the BC 
Ministry of Housing and Municipal Affairs . 
1.0  POLICY SECTION  
The City of Burnaby Housing Target Progress Report aligns with the provincial Housing 
Supply Act  (2023).  
Additionally, this report aligns with the following City policies and strategies:  
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021);  
• Burnaby’s Housing Needs Report (2021 , updated 2024) ; and  
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025 ). 
2.0  BACKGROUND  
The Housing Supply Act  came into effect in Spring 2023 . It enables the Province to 
establish housing targets for municipalities across British Columbia as part of a broader 
legislative framework intended to address the housing crisis.  Housing targets have been  
issued to municipalities on a staggered basis , and the City of Burnaby, as part of Group 
4, received its Housing Target Order on August 18, 2025  (see Attachment 1) .  
The Order sets a requirement for the City to deliver 10,240 net new housing units 
between September  1, 2025, and August 31, 2030 , which is derived based on meeting 
75% of the total Provincial Housing Needs Estimate for the City of Burnaby . The net new 
units are measured cumulatively with each reporting period, as illustrated in Table 1.

Under the Housing Supply Act , municipalities subject to  a Housing Target Order must 
submit regular progress reports to the Province. For Burnaby, the following progress 
reports are mandated over the five ‑year period : 
 
• Year 1a Report : September 1, 2025 – February 28, 2026 (6 -month interim report) ; 
• Year 1b Report : September 1, 2025 – August 31, 2026 ; 
• Year 2 Report : September 1, 2026 – August 31, 2027 ; 
• Year 3 Report : September 1, 2027 – August 31, 2028 ;  
• Year 4 Report: September 1, 2028 – August 31, 2029 ; and  
• Year 5 Report: September 1, 2029 – August 31, 2030 . 
 
The first six-month interim progress report is intended to provide the Province with an 
early indication of a municipality’s ability to meet or make progress toward achieving its 
housing targets. The report further describes actions taken by the municipality  to meet its 
housing targets, such as adoption of policies, plans, and bylaws that address housing 
needs.   
Each progress report must be received in a meeting that is open to the public and by 
Council resolution within 45 days following the end of its respective reporting period , 
which for Burnaby is April 14, 2026, for the current reporting period. Municipalities must  
subsequently submit  their progress report  to the Province  as soon as is practicable.   
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION  
Progress Towards Provincial Target  
For the 6-month interim reporting period  of September 1, 2025, to February 28, 2026, 
approximately 910 net new housing units have been completed within Burnaby.  
While the City is currently on track to meet its Year 1 Housing Target Order requirements, 
current economic conditions are creating uncertainty in the construction and delivery of 
new housing units with development starts having slowed across British Colum bia. The 
City has streamlined its development processes to facilitate timely reviews and approvals 
of new housing project. However, with housing units mainly constructed by the private 
market, the City ultimately does not control when housing will be compl eted, making it 
difficult for the City to control whether it can meet its Housing Target Order.  
In addition, the recent announcement to pause provincial funding programs for non -
market housing programs, including the Community Housing Fund (CHF), will have an 73
Subject: Provincial Housing Target Order 6 -Month Interim Progress Report  
Open Council Meeting  April 14, 2026  ................................ ...........  Page 3 
 
impact on municipalities, including the City of Burnaby, in delivering new housing units, 
especially affordable housing.  The program has historically supported a broad spectrum 
of affordable, purpose -built rental housing, including projects with deep levels of subsidy , 
such as Rent -Geared -to-Income (RGI) and shelter -rate units . This program has been a 
key mechanism for achieving the Province's long -term affordable housing supply targets, 
and many non-profit housing providers are reliant on this funding to m ove projects from 
planning to delivery . With no new funding anticipated for the foreseeable future,  a 
number of projects that had anticipated delivering deeply affordable rental units , such as 
Hall Towers which has 331 proposed units in Edmonds,  are now experiencing 
uncertainty regarding their timelines and viability.   
Burnaby’s Housing Target Progress Report Form is provided in Attachment 2 . This 
provides a  comprehensive summary of the housing units  delivered for this  six-month  
interim reporting  period, along with units currently in the development pipeline . It also 
provides additional information on the housing initiatives undertaken by the City in the 
past 12 months.  
4.0  COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
The Housing Target Progress R eport Form will be posted  on the City’s website and 
submitted to the Province to satisfy Housing Target Order requirements. The next 
provincial reporting deadline is August 31, 2026 , and thereafter annually until 20 30. 
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The completion and submission of the Housing Target Progress Report does not have a 
financial impact on the City. However, the delivery of housing units to meet the Housing 
Target Order placed on Burnaby, as well as the associated housing policies, plans a nd 
strategies needed to facilitate the delivery of new housing, will require resources , 
especially in relation to non -market housing  in the form of potential grants, fee waivers 
and staff support . In light of the funding pause from the provincial governmen t on its 
affordable housing funding program, it will be challenging for the City to achieve the 
Housing Target Order, particularly affordable units, without funding support from other 
orders of government. As such, it is finally noted that failure to achieve the Housing 
Target Order (non -market guideline) is a reflection of the Province’s inability to meet it’s 
own commitments, and not on the City’s ability to deliver such housing.  
 
Respectfully submitted,  
 
E. W. Kozak , General Manager Planning and Development

Methodological notes:  
 
• “Below Market Units,” as defined by the province, are interpreted in this report to be rent 
geared to income units, and the dataset has been organized accordingly throughout the 
analysis.  
• Secondary suites are currently tracked through Building Permits; however, there is no 
mechanism to confirm that legally authorized suites are being rented for residential purposes. 
This report includes all legal secondary suites that have been completed or  are currently under 
construction for this reporting period, with the assumption that they are being rented.  
• In Section 5, units recorded under “Rezoning” have received Final Adoption and have either 
not yet initiated a Building Permit application or  have a Building Permit under  review at the 
time of this Interim Report.  
• Unit breakdowns in Sections 3 and 5 do not align precisely with the unit totals. This is because 
the granular data entered in the City’s  tracking system may change as projects progress to the 
Building Permit stage. As a result, the unit totals, derived directly from permit data, represent 
the more accurate figures.  
• Only projects that have undergone a rezoning process are included in the unit breakdowns 
presented in Sections 3 and 5. Projects that proceed directly to the Building Permit stage, which 
include Small -scale Multi -unit Housing, are excluded from these break downs, as the City does 
not currently track granular unit data through permits.  
• Under the City of Burnaby Tenant Assistance Policy and Inclusionary Zoning regulation, some 
non-market rental units , provided as replacement rental units,  will revert to market rental at 
turnover; the City is working toward improving data collection to track these changes to ensure 
accurate data collection  over time .  
• For Master Plans currently underway across the City, only unit data related to site ‑specific 
rezonings that are in -stream have been included in this Interim Report. Many units in the City ’s 
Master Plans are part of future phases that are either currently advancing through the rezoning 
process or  have not yet been initiated.  
• In June 2024 , the City of Burnaby amended the Zoning Bylaw to permit Small  scale Multi  Unit 
Housing (SSMUH) within single and two -family  Zoning  Districts. Projects that applied for a 87

SUBJECT:  BUILDING BENCHMARK BC  
 
PURPOSE : To update Council on Building Benchmark BC and seek authorization to 
participate in Year 7 .  
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  staff be authorized to participate in Building Benchmark BC Year 7, as 
described in the report titled “Building Benchmark BC,” dated April 14, 2026, with 
no additional funding required beyond the existing 2026 budget . 
1.0  POLICY SECTION  
The City of Burnaby’s partnership with Building Benchmark BC  aligns with the following 
City plans and policies:  
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016) ; 
• Community Energy and Emissions Plan (2016) ; 
• Climate Action Framework  (2020) ; 
• City Energy Strategy (2020) ; 
• Zero Emission Building Retrofit Strategy (2022) ; and,  
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025).  
2.0  BACKGROUND  
Building Benchmark BC ( BBBC ) is a voluntary energy benchmarking and disclosure 
program launched in 2020 that has grown to involve more than 1,900 buildings and 
more than 90 organizations across British Columbia. It continues to be a collaborative 
initiative spearheaded by  local and regional governments, BC Hydro and the University 
of British Columbia , working with Open Technologies,  to promote climate change 
solutions in the built environment through collective action and scalable solutions  for 
informed dec ision-making . 
BBBC allows building owners and managers to measure, track and compare their 
buildings energy use and carbon emissions against similar properties, providing insights 
for operational improvements and retrofit opportunities.  The participating jurisdictions 
and partners also continue to explore the role of building energy benchmarking and 
disclosure within a broader framework of climate regulations, policies, and incentives.    
The City of Burnaby has been a local government partner of BBBC since its inception in 
2020. Council last received a report on BBBC in 2023 . Since that time, the City has 
continued  as a local government partner  by promoting the program to the public , 
leading by example by includ ing 30 civic buildings in the program  and, alongside other 
local government partners,  making an annual funding contribution  to support technical 
components, delivery and administration of the program.  90

3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION  
3.1  BBBC Annual Program Report  
Annual program reports, information about the program and the link to the public 
disclosure map can be  found at BBBC ’s website - www.buildingbenchmarkbc.ca .   
BBBC Annual Program Report 2024 (Year 5) is included as Attachment 1 for 
information.  Positive trends and outcomes  from the report include:   
• Overall trend of emissions reduction based on average emissions intensity of 
participating buildings  from Years 1 to 5;   
• Growth in participating buildings to 1,919  in Year 5  (from 765 in Year 1) and 15 
million square feet of floor space; in Burnaby, participating buildings and square 
footage have more than tripled since Year 1;  and 
• Award for Best Improvement of Energy Performance given to Lochdale 
Elementary School (Burnaby Schools – School District  41). 
The annual s core cards prepared for Burnaby civic buildings have shown  notable 
improvements in building performance for buildings as they have undergone energy 
and/or emissions retrofits.  
The Year 6 report is not yet available.   
3.2  BBBC Year 7  
2026 will be Year 7 of BBB C. Buildings already in the program will receive information 
about their energy use and emissions from the previous calendar year , benchmarked 
against other similar buildings  for comparison . Owners and managers of buildings 
recruited to join BBBC for the first time will shar e their building’s energy use for the 
current year.  BBBC will continue to advocate for provincial leadership to develop 
mandatory energy reporting and benchmarking programs for existing buildings, similar to 
other jurisdictions in Canada, the USA and around world. In the meantime, BBBC members 
City of Victoria , District of Saanich and the Capital Regional District  (CRD)  are advancing 
their own  regional energy reporting program for large buildings beginning in 2026 . 
3.3  Burnaby Participation in  BBBC Year 7  
For Burnaby , continuing our partnership with BBBC aligns with the City Energy Strategy 
and energy management of existing buildings to improve efficiency and low carbon 
performance. The partnership also supports the  Climate Action Framework and  Zero 
Emission Building Retrofit Strategy , specifically Actions 3.4 and 4.1 that focus o n 
advancing policy and regulatio n to requir e energy reporting , similar to the program in 
the CRD  region , and advocat ing for leadership from regional and provincial 
governments.  Through the City’s continued partnership with BBBC, enrolled privately 
and publicly owned  buildings in Burnaby have access to  free energy -use and emissions 
data to inform the  retrofit decision -making an d strategic energy management  needed to 
reducing building emissions from existing buildings.   
With Council authorization of this report, the City will continue its participation in Building 
Benchmark BC Year 7, with associated costs to be accommodated within the Planning 
and Development Department’s existing 2026 budget.  91

Building Benchmark BC is a collaborative initiative 
spearheaded by local and regional governments, BC 
Hydro and the University of British Columbia that 
aims to promote climate change solutions in the built 
environment through collective action and innovation 
solutions. The participating jurisdictions and partners 
are exploring the role of building energy benchmarking 
and disclosure within a broader framework of climate 
regulations, policies, and incentives.
The program is managed and administered by OPEN 
Green Building Society, an independent non-profit that 
works closely with OPEN Technologies on research 
and the delivery of benchmarking programs. OPEN 
Technologies, is a private software company that developed and maintains GRID, the data visualization 
platform central to Building Benchmark BC. OPEN is also 
contracted to provide services to Building Benchmark 
BC. We are grateful for the support of our partners who 
have provided funding or in-kind resources to make this 
program possible.

Cadillac Fairview completed Canada’s first  geo-
exchange retrofit of an occupied, mixed-use 
 high-rise complex in downtown Vancouver with 
 minimal disruption to occupants.
Partnering with Fenix Energy, the system, finished in 2014, 
captures and stores waste heat from the 19-floor office tower 
and Pacific Centre retail space, reducing steam usage by 85% 
in the first year and cutting approximately 658 tonnes of CO₂ 
annually. The system uses advanced water-cooled chillers, heat 
recovery modules, and a geothermal plant, with 30 boreholes 
drilled in a confined parkade space using innovative vertical drilling 
technology. Completed in six months through coordinated efforts, 
the project achieved significant energy savings and emissions 
reductions, showcasing the potential for geo-exchange retrofits in 
large existing buildings.Year Completed

OSO Development 
GOLDEN, BC
Showing that zero carbon doesn’t just happen in 
the big city, we showcased OSO, a development in 
Golden completed in 2023.
OSO is a mixed-use development featuring two buildings with 
commercial and residential units, designed for cost efficiency 
and climate resilience. Vidorra’s expertise in construction cost 
management contributed to achieving airtightness of 0.6 ACH 
using strategic structural designs. The all-electric mechanical 
system includes high-efficiency energy recovery ventilators 
(ERVs) with 85% heat recovery, Mitsubishi City Multi heat pumps 
providing heating even at -25°C, and SANCO2 heat pump water 
heaters integrated with Stiebel Eltron tankless systems.
To enhance tenant comfort amid climate change, the centralized 
ventilation system provides active mechanical cooling, with MERV 
13 filters for air purification and optional carbon filtration for 
forest fire smoke. Cost-effective construction strategies included 
extensive energy modeling, a single utility meter for commercial 
rates, and eliminating gas infrastructure and related costs.Manager / Owner

The Need for 
Provincial Leadership 
in Benchmarking & 
DisclosureTo fully realize the potential of building energy benchmarking, 
it is time for the Province of British Columbia to take decisive 
leadership by making benchmarking and disclosure mandatory 
across the province. 
Building Benchmark BC: Reducing 
Emissions and Boosting Efficiency
Building Benchmark BC has shown that 
whether you are a social housing provider , 
commercial property owner , school board, 
or local government, benchmarking and 
reporting is now widely accepted. What is 
more is that over 95% of participants in the 
program are choosing to voluntarily disclose 
their energy and emissions publicly.
What is also clear is that not only is 
benchmarking and reporting happening, it’s 
helping. The average GHG intensity of the 
buildings in Building Benchmark BC has been consistently dropping over the five years of the 
program. When we look at the more than 160 
office buildings that participate in our program 
we have seen an average of a 10% drop in 
GHG intensity. We have seen even larger 
GHG reductions in our Multi-family sample 
of buildings where we have 297 participating 
buildings. These results are consistent with 
research from the United States where we 
have seen that benchmarking and reporting 
programs routinely and predictably result in 
energy savings and lower emissions. 95%of participants in the program are 
choosing to voluntarily disclose 
their energy and emissions publicly
Building Benchmark BC Annual Report 2024
15108
We also have seen that benchmarking and 
reporting has gone from being an innovative 
policy (implemented by leaders like New 
York and Seattle over a decade ago) into the 
mainstream. The Province of Ontario has 
had a requirement for more than 5 years, 
and the City of Vancouver is in its second 
year of Energize Vancouver . Other BC local 
governments are considering their own 
programs such as the City of Richmond, the 
City of Victoria, and the District of Saanich.
Many jurisdictions are also now moving 
beyond basic benchmarking to mandatory 
performance standards that require buildings 
to take action in reducing their energy use 
and emissions. Vancouver , New York, Seattle, 
Washington DC and Madison (Wisconsin) 
all have some kind of policy that requires 
a portion of the buildings in their reporting programs to improve their performance 
annually.
The Province of BC is falling behind on climate 
action, and while cities stepping into this 
void should be commended, it is leading to 
a fracturing of the regulatory environment 
as it pertains to existing buildings. Different 
reporting requirements with different 
processes and different schedules are 
emerging all over the province which is more 
difficult for industry to manage and leads 
to less optimal outcomes. This is because 
data collection, and by extension, the data 
sets themselves also become fragmented. 
All of this results in increased effort, more 
confusion, and less useful results than if the 
data were to be collected at scale under one 
provincial program.The Need for Mandatory Benchmarking 
& Performance Standards in BC
Seattle, Washington
New York, New York Madison, Wisconsin“Vancouver, New York, Seattle, Washington DC and 
Madison (Wisconsin) all have some kind of policy that 
requires a portion of the buildings in their reporting 
programs to improve their performance annually.”

16
109
Provincial leadership would provide consistent 
regulations across British Columbia, enabling 
building owners and managers to participate 
effectively and equitably. It would also better 
empower local governments to align with 
provincial climate goals while ensuring better 
access to more consistent data for both the 
Provincial government, the utilities, and the 
industry as whole. Finally this would result in 
significant cost savings and energy savings for 
the industry in general and most importantly 
for many renters, apartment owners, and 
small businesses.There is also no better prepared jurisdiction 
to implement benchmarking and reporting 
at the provincial level than the Province of 
British Columbia. This is due in part to capacity 
building programs like building benchmark 
BC, but mostly because BC is fortunate to 
have some of the most progressive utilities 
anywhere in North America when it comes 
to benchmarking and reporting. Both major 
utilities have billing and data integrations 
with Energy Star Portfolio Manager . These 
two utilities serve between them more than 
90% of customers in the province and have years supporting their customers through 
the benchmarking process. Many jurisdictions 
that currently require benchmarking still do 
not have the levels of automation and access 
that both BC utilities currently provide. This 
support is also evident in the number of 
buildings entered into Energy Star Portfolio 
Manager . According to Natural Resources 
Canada as of December 2022, BC has 5,300 
buildings in ESPM which is second only to 
Ontario with 13,000.The Case for Provincial Leadership in

That is why this year the Open Green Building 
Society and our local government partners 
in Building Benchmark BC are calling on 
the Provincial Government to implement a 
benchmarking and reporting requirement 
for commercial and multi-family buildings in 
2026 that reports on 2025 consumption data. 
The proposed regulation could be phased in 
starting with large buildings over 200,000 
square feet in the first year , and then stepping 
down to buildings over 100,000 square feet 
in the second year , and finally buildings over 
50,000 square feet in the third year .
The reporting deadlines could be negotiated 
with industry but be informed by the City of 
Vancouver’s current reporting schedule.“The proposed regulation

SUBJECT:  7950 PANDORA ST  & 1200 GAGLARDI WAY – BURNABY MOUNTAIN  
– STATUTORY RIGHT -OF-WAY FOR FORTISBC  
 
PURPOSE : To seek Council approval to grant Statutory Right -of-Way in favour of 
FortisBC  Energy Inc. (FortisBC) over a portion of 7950 Pandora St . and 
1200 Gaglardi Way  to permit the installation, operations and 
maintenance of natural gas  works.  
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the granting and execution of Statutory Right -of-Way over a portion of 
7950 Pandora St . and 1200 Gaglardi Way in favor of FortisBC Energy Inc. for the 
installation, operations, and maintenance of infrastructure required to service 
Burnaby Mountain Conservation Area, as outlined in the report titled “7950 
Pandora St & 1200 Gaglardi Way – Burnaby Mountain – Statutory Right -of-Way 
for FortisBC”, dat ed April 14, 2026, be approved.  
1.0 POLICY SECTION  
The expansion of new services  in the Burnaby Mountain Conservation Area and  the 
granting of the associated Statutory Right -of-Way is aligned and supported by the 
following Council adopted policies and plans/strategies:  
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) and,  
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan  (2025) . 
2.0 BACKGROUND  
As part of the improvements and renovations at the Mintara restaurant within the 
Burnaby Mountain Conservation Area,  City staff have been working with FortisBC to 
identify infrastructure required to provide natural gas service to the park facilities. 
FortisBC has identified the need to access a portion of the property at 1200 Gaglardi 
Way, as shown as SRW # 1 on Attachment 1  – Sketch 2, to accommodate the 
installation of new natural gas infrastructure.  
During the review of the existing natural gas infrastructure along University Drive, 
FortisBC discovered that the gas main is currently not protected by a Statutory Right -of-
Way (“SRW”). The gas main was constructed in the 1960s to service the Simon Fraser  
University (“SFU”) campus. Since the discovery, FortisBC has been working with SFU 
and the City to obtain a SRW over the existing gas main. While the majority of the gas 
main is located on SFU lands, FortisBC require access to a small portion of 7950 114

To permit the installation, operations, and maintenance of the required infrastructure,

The City has not requested compensation for SRWs as the FortisBC infrastructure is 
necessary to provide natural gas services  to facilities within the Burnaby Mountain 
Conservation Area.

No community engagement is required as part of this SRW approval request.  
4.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
There are no additional City costs associated with the preparation and execution of this 
SRW.

DEPARTMENT(S)  LANDS AND FACILITIES   
  
SUBJECT:  CA - DEVELOPMENT  OF MODULAR HOUSING FOUR -PLEX 
UNIT AT  8315 14TH AVENUE  
 
PURPOSE : To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the 
development of a modular four -plex housing unit as a pilot project . 
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  a contract award to Echelon Creative Inc.  for an estimated cost of $1,354,463  
including  taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CA – Development of Modular Housing 
Four-Plex Unit at 8315 – 14th Avenue ” dated  April 14, 2026 , be approved; and  
THAT  final payment will be based on actual quantities of goods delivered and unit 
prices quoted . 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY  
This recommendation is to approve a contract award in the amount of $1,354,463  including tax  
to Echelon Creative Inc. for this development project.  
The project will demonstrate the feasibility and cost effectiveness of standardized modular 
construction for small scale multi -unit housing in low density neighbo urhoods . This pilot project  
forms part of a broader initiative supported by Modular BC and the National Research Council 
to advance low-carbon  construction methods and scalable housing solutions .  The anticipated 
project delivery is approximately 6 – 8 months and typically, modular housing has an expected 
useful life of about 50 to 60 years. With p roper maintenance and periodic upgrades, the lifespan 
can extend beyond 80 years.  
Echelon Creative Inc. will act as the builder  and be  responsible for the modular unit design, 
fabrication, delivery, installation and coordination of trades to complete the modular building. 
The City will be responsible for overall project oversight including site development works and 
site preparation while the Burnaby Housing Authority will provide additional project support 
through  coordination of activities and process documentation/performance metrics to help 
establish best practices for future project s. The City will retain  ownership of both the land and 
the completed building, which will be leased as part of the City’s residential rental portfolio.   
Preliminary cost estimates associated with the City’s scope of work have been established; 
however, staff are still in the process of finalizing these estimates  with the goal of keeping costs 
as low as possible .  
 120
Subject: CA- Development of Modular Housing Four -Plex Unit at 8315 – 14th Avenue  
Open Council Meeting  April 14, 2026 ................................ ............  Page 2 
 
1.0 POLICY SECTION  
The City’s Procurement Policy requires that contracts for goods, services, and construction 
with values over $500,000 be approved by Council following a competitive procurement 
process.  
2.0 BACKGROUND  
The City of Burnaby is participating in a modular housing pilot initiative intended to evaluate 
the use of standardized factory -built construction for small -scale , multi -unit housing 
developments. The project site is located at 8315 14th Avenue.  
The pilot project is being undertaken in collaboration with Modular BC and forms part of the 
National Research Council’s Platform to Decarbonize the Construction Sector at Scale.  
The proposed building will include:  
• Approximately 5,319 sq ft of gross floor area  
• Four residential units  
• Three  storeys  
• One accessible dwelling unit  
• Private outdoor space for each unit  
The pilot will provide data and lessons learned that can inform future modular housing 
developments within Burnaby and across the province.  
Architectural drawings have been  prepared and the design generally complies with Burnaby 
zoning requirements. Preliminary geotechnical review indicates the site is suitable for 
conventional foundation systems.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
The estimated total contract value is $ 1,354,463  including taxes.  
Under the proposed delivery model, Burnaby Housing Authority, in coordination with City staff, 
will oversee procurement, construction oversight, and project delivery.   This 
approach  leverages  off-site construction to improve schedule certainty, cost contro l, and 
construction efficiency while  maintaining  required quality and regulatory compliance.   
 
As a modular housing supplier and builder, Echelon Creative Inc. will  be responsible for:    
 
• Design development and coordination of modular units ;  
• Off-site fabrication  of modular units ;   
• Transportation and delivery of  modular units  to site ;  
• Installation, assembly, and commissioning of the modular units ;  
• Coordination of trades for mechanical, electrical, and finishing works ; and  
• Quality control and compliance with applicable codes and standards .

Not applicable  
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
This project was not included in the approved 2026 –2030 Financial Plan. Should  Council 
choose to proceed, interim funding  to support this contract award for the supply and install of 
the modular building  would be provided from contingency while staff assess where funding 
could be reallocated within the existing 2026 plan.

At the November 21, 2022 Council Meeting, Council directed staff to proceed with the 
detailed design of Option 1 for the proposed Burnaby Lake Overpass, which connects to 
the trailhead at Glencarin Drive at the north landing and existing park space near 
Claude Avenue and McCarthy Court on the south landing. The City has retained 
McElhanney Ltd. with Fast + Epp and VIA Architecture as the lead design team along 
with Jacobs Bros Construction as the Construction Manager.  
The Burnaby Lake Overpass is now  complete and the grand opening is scheduled for  
April 26, 2026 including  a public event for the local community, interested parties, 
dignitaries and media . Residents have informally begun using the overpass.  
Although policy outlines the ECC should review the wording and content of 
commemorative plaques, due to the timing of the opening event, approval is being 
requested by Council. The next ECC meeting is scheduled for May 5, 2026.  149

Council .  
1.0   POLICY SECTION  
The proposed rezoning application is consistent with the following policies and plans 
adopted by Council:  
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022),  
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan ( 2025 ), 
• Royal Oak Community Plan (2025) , 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007),  
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011),  
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016),  
• Transportation Plan (2021),  
• Burnaby District Energy Policy (2024), and  
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021) . 
2.0  BACKGROUND  
2.1 The subject development site is located along Beresford Street, between 
Dunblane Ave and Burlington Avenue , and is designated with a base designation 
of Mid -Rise Apartment 2 in the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (see 
Attachment 1 ). The subject site is located within the Royal Oak Community Plan 
area and is comprised of 1 lot, which is currently improved with a printing shop.  
2.2 The applicant has submitted a plan of development in alignment with the R6 Mid -
Rise Apartment 2 District guidelines, that is suitable for Council’s consideration of 
First and Second Reading of the Rezoning Bylaw.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
3.1 The development proposal, as shown in Figure 1 , is for a 20 -storey residential 
apartment building with an adjoining 9 -storey terraced podium. The proposed 
development includes 215 apartment dwelling units. Vehicular access to the 
underground parking is provided via the rear lane from Dunblane Avenue. 
Pedestrian access is provided from Beresford Street. This proposal includes 
indoor and outdoor rooftop amenity space. A more detailed summary of the 
proposed development, including residential unit mix, amenity space, and parking 
and loading, is provided i n Attachment 2.

height and setbacks associated with the R6 District. The permitted and proposed 
building height for the subject site are outlined in Table 1 .

averaging or community benefit bonus is pursued, the subject rezoning application 
does not propose any additional height and  under the subject rezoning  is limited to 
the base permitted height of 20 storeys.  
It is noted that the proposed development includes a rooftop amenity space. In 
accordance with Section 6.4.1.1(3) of the Zoning Bylaw, rooftop access, 
mechanical penthouses, and elevator overruns are excluded from the height 
calculation. The proposal meets  these conditions and is therefore considered a 20 -
storey building in accordance with the Zoning Bylaw.  
3.4 The applicant has the option to pursue rental or strata housing under the R6 
District and has indicated their intention to pursue rental housing. In accordance 
with the Inclusionary Rental Requirements under the Zoning Bylaw, inclusionary 
rental requireme nts do not apply to market rental developments. The applicant will 
be required to confirm  the final tenure  of the development  prior to Preliminary Plan 
Approval/ Development Permit issuance. Should a strata  development  be pursued 
instead of market renta l dwelling units, the development will be required to provide 
non-market inclusionary rental units equivalent to 10% of the total number of units 
proposed (excluding any market rental units), in line with the Council -adopted 
inclusionary requirements. The registration of a Housing Covenant and a Housing 
Agreement will be required to protect and regulate the affordabilit y and tenure of 
any required non -market inclusionary rental units. A Section 219 Covenant will be 
required at Final Adoption of the Rezoning  Bylaw outlining the requirements for 
housing tenure and associated inclusionary requirements to be established prior to 
Preliminary Plan Approval/Development Permit issuance, and Council approval of 
the Housing Agreement Bylaw, if applicable, will be required be fore Building Permit 
Issuance. Any refinements to strata or rental tenure must  ensure unit size 
adjustments and any other necessary adjustments comply with the then -current 
Zoning Bylaw.  
3.5 As the subject site is located within a transit -oriented development area, off -street 
residential parking spaces (other than accessible parking spaces) are not required. 
A residential parking ratio of 0.2 3 spaces per unit is voluntarily proposed for the 
development, including 11 accessible spaces and 1 2 visitor spaces. A more 
detailed summary of proposed parking including bicycle parking is outlined in 
Attachment 2 .  
In accordance with the Transportation Demand Management Guidelines for sites 
located within a transit -oriented development area, the applicant is providing  a 
suite of transportation demand management strategies for residents, which 
include:  
• a contribution equal to $500 per dwelling unit toward a car -share driving credit 
program, to be pooled and allocated to participating residents over time;  
• a contribution equal to $1,000 per dwelling unit toward a transit pass program, 
to be pooled and allocated to participating residents over time ; 158

options.  
The proposed parking rates may be varied prior to the issuance of a Preliminary

with all necessary covenants secured to ensure delivery of the artwork or cash -in-
