COUNCIL REPORT  
 
TO: MEM BERS OF COU NCIL 
 
FROM:  MAYOR MIKE HURLEY  
SUBJECT:  UPDATE ON MAYOR’S TASK FORCE ON BURNABY  ECONOMIC 
GROWTH AND  RESILIENCE  
 
PURPOSE : To update members of Council on the progress of the Mayor’s Task 
Force on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience.  
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the report  titled “Update on Mayor’s Task Force on Burnaby Economic 
Growth and Resilience” dated April 14, 2026, be  receive d for information.  
1.0 POLICY SECTION  
In accordance with the Community Charter  (Division 4, Section 142) ‘Select Committees 
of Council’, Council may establish and appoint a select committee to consider or inquire 
into any matter and to report its findings and opinion to Council.  
2.0 BACKGROUND  
On September 9, 2025, Council approved the establishment of the Mayor’s Task Force 
on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience (the “Task Force”), including its Terms of 
Reference, as a select committee of Council. The Task Force was created to bring 
togethe r a cross ‑section of business and community leaders to explore Burnaby’s 
economic strengths and challenges and to develop informed, actionable 
recommendations to support long ‑term economic growth and resilience.  
Task Force members were appointed by Council on December 9, 2025. The Task Force 
commenced its work in January 2026 and has been meeting through a series of 
bi‑weekly, facilitator ‑supported workshops , to discuss  and identify emerging themes 
through structured dialogue.  
This report provides Council with an interim update on the Task Force’s progress at the 
mid‑point of its work program, prior to the completion of its deliberations and the 
development of formal recommendations for Council’s consideration.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
3.1 Workshop series  
The Task Force commenced its work on January 8, 2026 and has met five times 
through facilitator -supported workshop s to date . Workshops generally begin with an 
introductory discussion to frame the session topic, followed by presentations from guest 174
Subject: Update on Mayor’s Task Force on  Burnaby  Economic Growth and Resilience  
Open Council Meeting  April 14, 2026  ................................ ...........  Page 2 
 
lecturers to provide insight into the broader economic landscape . Members then 
participate in  facilitated workshop sessions guided by a series of focused questions.  
Guest presentations  introduced key themes , including  economic development in the 
municipal, regional and national context , as well as economic resilience and inclusive 
economic practices . Building on  these presentations , members engaged in facilitated 
discussions to examine  how these themes apply locally  in Burnaby and to identify  
potential areas of strategic focus.  
A key milestone was reached during the most recent workshop on March 5, 2026, with 
the identification of strategic priorities intended to guide the development of 
recommendations to Council.  Following this milestone, the Task Force paused its 
workshop meetings to allow for validation of the identified strategic priorities through 
targeted engagement, including surveys and interviews with the business community 
and key industry stakeholders who  were not present at the workshop . The Task Force is 
scheduled to  reconvene on April 16, 2026 to continue its work.  
3.2 Emerging themes  
Discussions  emphasized  the importance of creating the right conditions for businesses  
in sectors where Burnaby already demonstrates strength , as well as  support ing 
businesses  through their full life cycle,  from start ‑up to expansion and retention .  
Members also discussed  the role the City could pla y in convening partners and aligning 
efforts  to foster  a more coordinated  and collaborative  economic ecosystem .  
Given Burnaby ’s position as one of the most diverse cities in Western Canada, diversity  
was recognized  as a key economic asset, with a focus on  reducing barriers and 
expanding access for underrepresented groups. There was also recognition for the 
need to  protect and strengthen the foundations of the local economy through resilient 
infrastructure and emergency preparedness . Collectively, t hese discussions  inform ed 
the development of strategic priorities to be validated with the business community and 
key stakeholder groups.  
3.3 Next Steps  
At the time of writing , survey  results  are being c ompiled and analyze d. The Task Force 
will reconvene for three additional  sessions between April 16 to end of May 2026 to 
confirm the strategic priorities and develop actionable recommendations for 
present ation  to Council on June 23, 2026.  
4.0 COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
Engagement with the business community was undertaken through a survey and 10 
targeted stakeholder interview s conducted  between March 13 and April 2 , 2026 .  This 
engagement was intended  to inform and refine the draft strategic priorities.  
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
There are no financial considerations  associated with  this report.  175

Mayor Mike Hurley

COUNCIL REPORT  
 
TO: MAYOR AND COUNCILLORS  
 
FROM : GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT   
  
SUBJECT:  AMENITY PIER COVENANT – DEVELOPER ELECTION TO FORFEIT 
SECURITY  
 
PURPOSE : To provide Council with information regarding the owner’s election under 
a Section 219 Covenant registered on title at 8351 Fraser Reach Court 
to forfeit the posted security and not proceed with construction of the 
public amenity pier contemplated under th e Riverbend Business Park 
rezoning approvals, and the City’s holding of the forfeited security 
pending future Council direction . 
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT the report titled “ Amenity Pier Covenant – Developer Election to Forfeit 
Security ” dated April 14, 2026 , be received for information.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION  
The following report aligns with provincial regulation and municipal bylaws and policies, 
including : 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) ; 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan ( 2025 ); and,  
• Economic Development Strategy (2007) . 
2.0  BACKGROUND  
In 2016, Council approved REZ #12-18, which established a multi -phased Conceptual 
Master Plan and design guidelines for Oxford Properties’  Riverbend Business Park, 
located at 8211, 8220, 8340, and 8351 Fraser Reach Court (see Attachment 1  – 
Sketch #1). The Conceptual Plan included a detailed  public open space concept that 
centered  around the site’s adjacency to the Fraser River, with the goal of providing 
numerous points of access to the foreshore and an amenity pier on the Fraser River.  
One of the requirements established under the Riverbend Business Park Master Plan,  
and two site-specific rezoning  applications  for the business park ( REZ# 16-50 and 
REZ#18-33), was the provision of a public amenity pier in the Fraser River.  The pier 
was intended to connect to and complement the waterfront trail located within a 
statutory right -of-way, which in turn links to the broader Burnaby Fraser Foreshore Park 
and accommodates both pedestria ns and cyclists.  While a portion of the  amenity  pier 
was to be  located on 8340 Fraser Reach Court, t he majority was proposed to be located 177
Subject: Amenity Pier Covenant – Developer Election to Forfeit Security  
Open Council  Meeting April 14, 2026  ................................ ...........  Page 2 
 
within a water lot owned by the Province. Construction and operation within this area 
required a Crown Land Tenure from the Province in the form of a Nominal Rent Tenure 
(NRT) Lease.  
As part of the rezoning approval for REZ#18 -33, a Section 219 Covenant was 
registered on title, restricting Final Occupancy of the new two -storey light industrial 
building at 8351 Fraser Reach Court until the identified amenity pier was constructed.  
However, b ecause  Oxford Properties wished to obtain occupancy of  their approved 
buildings  prior to satisfying the conditions set out in th at Section 219 Covenant , a new 
Section 219 Covenant was registered against 8351 Fraser Reach Court . The covenant 
provides that if the owner is unable to obtain the required Crown Land Tenure, the 
owner may elect to forfeit the security in lieu of constructing the amenity pier. The 
covenant also provides that the City may use the forfeited security toward  construction 
of the pier or an alternative public amenity along the Fraser Foreshore or elsewhere in 
the City . 
On December 13, 2021, Council authorized staff to submit the required provincial 
applications for the NRT Lease and to prepare a Construction, Operation and 
Maintenance Agreement with the property owner . On September 9th, 2025, Council 
received a n information  report that  provided an update on the public amenity pier, 
including that it was anticipated that , subject to approval of the NRT Lease and 
completion of relevant City applications and processes,  construction could  potentially  
begin in late 2025 . 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION  
Since th at report was received by Council , Oxford Properties continued to work with the 
City and Province  to develop plans for the amenity pier and obtain the required Crown 
Land Tenure  from the Province . On December 19th, 2025, the property owner advised  
the City that it had been unable to secure the required Crown Land Tenure from the 
Province and has exercised its option under the covenant to forfeit the posted security 
in lieu of proceeding with construction of the amenity pier . In accordance with the terms 
of the Section 219 Covenant , security in the amount of $1,363,500 wil l be forfeited to 
the City, as acknowledged by the owner , and per 3.1(e) of the covenant, the “City may 
use the Security to secure completion of all or a portion of the design, construction, 
installation and completion of the Amenity Pier Works or an alternative amenity as 
deemed appropriate by the City in an alternate location  along the Fraser Foreshore or 
elsewhere within the City of Burnaby. ” 
Under the terms of the Section 219 Covenant, where the owner elects to forfeit the 
posted security in lieu of constructing the amenity pier, the City is required to discharge 
the covenant from title once the forfeited security has been received. Planning w ill 
provide instructions to the Legal Department to discharge the covenant accordingly . 
Retention of the $1,363,500 security ensures that funds originally secured to deliver a 
public amenity remain available to support future public amenity improvements 
consistent with the intent of the covenant . The owner has advised that their preference 178
Subject: Amenity Pier Covenant – Developer Election to Forfeit Security  
Open Council  Meeting April 14, 2026  ................................ ...........  Page 3 
 
is to provide payment in the amount of $1,363,500 and  have the letters of credit 
provided as security returned to them upon receipt.  
Although the amenity pier will not move forward due to the complexity of water lot 
approvals and engineering requirements, public access to the Fraser River remains in 
place. The existing waterfront trail continues to provide safe and direct access for 
pedestrians and cyclists, and the public will continue to enjoy the river frontage. While 
the pier would have added another feature, the core public access along the foreshore 
is secured.  
The forfeited security will be held by the City and may be applied toward the design or 
construction of a public amenity in the City, consistent with the terms of the covenant. 
Consideration of the future use of these funds will be addressed through the Ci ty’s 
capital planning process.  
4.0  COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
Not applicable . 
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
In accordance with the terms of the Section 219 Covenant, the City will retain the 
security in the amount of $1,363,500. These funds are not currently allocated within the 
2026 -2030 Financial Plan, and the City will have some flexibility in how they may be 
used  for amenities . The monies received will be deposited into the City’s Capital Asset 
Works Financing R eserve Fund (CAWFF ) where they can be utilized  for public 
amenities . Consideration of how the funds may be allocated will occur through a future 
capit al planning process, at which time Council may determine the most appropriate use 
consistent with the intent of the covenant.  
 
Respectfully submitted,  
E. W. Kozak , General Manager Planning and Development

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby March 24, 2026 Meeting Minutes Up for Adoption
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on adopting the draft minutes from its March 24, 2026 meeting at the upcoming April 14, 2026 meeting. That March meeting covered several decisions relevant to business owners, including rezoning proposals for mixed-use commercial and residential developments, a new self-storage facility, contract awards for city services, and final adoption of bylaws updating parking meter rules and city fees and charges.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the March 24 meeting decisions — especially rezoning and fee bylaw changes — to see if your business is affected.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider Letter Urging Federal Action on Chlorine Rail Transport Safety
agenda_briefs.summary: At a Special Council meeting on April 1, 2026, Burnaby Council voted unanimously to have Mayor Hurley write a letter to the federal Minister of Transport raising serious safety concerns about liquid chlorine being transported by rail through densely populated areas of the Lower Mainland. The concern stems from a nearby chlorine plant in North Vancouver that ships an average of four rail cars of liquid chlorine through Burnaby every day — a risk that presenters say has not been fully assessed.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If your business is near a Burnaby rail corridor, monitor council updates on chlorine transport safety measures.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider: Declaring April 28th as Official Day of Mourning for Workers
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hear a delegation from the New Westminster & District Labour Council requesting that April 28th be officially declared the 'Day of Mourning for Workers Killed and Injured on the Job.' The group is also asking Council to observe a minute of silence at the April 14 meeting and to encourage city staff to do the same at 11:00 a.m. on April 28th.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If worker safety matters to your business, attend the April 14 Council meeting to hear the presentation.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council delegation — Day of Mourning presentation
Day of Mourning for Workers — minute of silence observed

agenda_briefs.industries: Labour & Employment, Construction & Development, Professional Services

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on appointing new Chief Election Officer for Burnaby's 2026 election
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on appointing Dennis Back as the new Chief Election Officer for the October 17, 2026 General Local Election. The previous officer, Samantha Pellizzari, is on leave and is not expected to return before election day, so a replacement is needed to keep election planning on track.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this affects who will run Burnaby's 2026 municipal election.

key_dates.label: Burnaby 2026 General Local Election day

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider: North Road BIA Renewal Blocked by Property Owner Opposition
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report showing that enough property owners on North Road voted against renewing the Business Improvement Area (BIA) — a special zone where businesses pay an extra tax to fund local improvements — to stop the renewal from moving forward. Over 55% of affected property owners (representing 52% of total property value) submitted formal opposition, meeting the legal threshold required to block the bylaw. Council will consider accepting this result, which means the North Road BIA will not be renewed and the association will not receive grant funding for 2026–2027.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own or operate a business on North Road, watch for updates on whether the BIA attempts a new renewal bid in fall 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal — earliest possible new Council initiative attempt

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby Housing Authority annual governance and board appointments for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on routine annual housekeeping matters for the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) — a city-owned housing company. If approved, Council would skip a formal annual general meeting (AGM), appoint KPMG as auditor, confirm December 12, 2026 as the official reference date, accept the BHA's 2025 financial statements, and formally appoint 10 directors to the BHA board for either two- or three-year terms.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this affects how Burnaby's city-owned housing corporation is governed in 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Housing Authority annual reference date — BHA corporate governance

agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Finance & Insurance

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Housing Authority 2025 Financials and 2026 Budget
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive the Burnaby Housing Authority's (BHA) first-ever audited financial statements and its 2026 operating budget at the April 14, 2026 meeting. Accepting these documents would unlock $2 million in City operating funding for the BHA in 2026, supporting two new rental housing projects — one at 10th Avenue & Newcombe Street (204 units) and one at Marine Drive in Edmonds (183 townhomes).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for Council's decision — BHA housing projects may affect local construction and property markets.

key_dates.label: BHA $25M GIC investment matures — Burnaby Housing Authority

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on bylaw changes for e-bike share and e-scooter programs in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on amendments to three city bylaws that would set the rules for a new shared e-bike rental program and bring city rules in line with BC's provincial e-scooter pilot (which allows privately owned electric kick scooters on Burnaby streets). The proposed changes would define where e-bikes and e-scooters can ride and park, create designated parking zones for shared e-bikes, and set fines of $200 for riding these devices in areas not approved for their use.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — new riding and parking rules for e-bikes and e-scooters could affect your business operations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby e-bike share and e-scooter bylaw amendments — council vote

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on submitting Burnaby's first provincial housing target progress report
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on authorizing staff to submit Burnaby's first 6-month progress report under BC's Housing Supply Act, which requires the city to build 10,240 net new homes by 2030. The report shows 910 net new units were completed between September 2025 and February 2026, but warns that slowing construction starts and a pause in provincial affordable housing funding could make future targets harder to hit.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — funding cuts to affordable housing programs may affect development timelines in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: BC Housing Supply Act — Year 1b progress report submission deadline
Burnaby Development Permit system (Form & Character, Streamside, Tenant Protection) takes effect

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby rejoining Building Benchmark BC energy tracking program for Year 7
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on whether to continue the city's participation in Building Benchmark BC (BBBC) for Year 7 (2026). BBBC is a voluntary program that helps building owners track and compare their energy use and carbon emissions. No new money is needed — the estimated $16,500 cost would come from the Planning and Development Department's existing 2026 budget.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own a building in Burnaby, consider signing up for BBBC to get free energy and emissions data at buildingbenchmarkbc.ca.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Building Benchmark BC Year 7 participation approval

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on: FortisBC natural gas right-of-way at Burnaby Mountain Conservation Area
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on granting FortisBC Energy Inc. a statutory right-of-way — a legal permission to access and use a strip of city-owned land — over portions of 7950 Pandora St. and 1200 Gaglardi Way. This would allow FortisBC to install, operate, and maintain natural gas infrastructure needed to service the Mintara restaurant and other facilities in the Burnaby Mountain Conservation Area.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.35M contract for modular four-plex housing pilot in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a $1,354,463 contract to Echelon Creative Inc. to build a modular four-plex (a factory-built, four-unit residential building) at 8315 14th Avenue as a pilot project. The project is designed to test whether this style of construction is a cost-effective way to add small-scale rental housing in lower-density neighbourhoods.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 14, 2026 council decision — it could signal new modular housing opportunities in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — modular four-plex contract award decision

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $2.4M contract to replace Jerry Rogers Creek culvert in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on awarding a $2,404,076 contract to HANNA Infrastructure Ltd. to replace an aging, undersized culvert (a pipe or tunnel that carries water under a road or structure) at Jerry Rogers Creek with a larger box culvert built to handle major flood events. The project is already funded through the City's 2026–2030 capital plan, with money coming from federal gas tax reserves and drainage development fees.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 14, 2026 council decision if your business operates near Jerry Rogers Creek or Marine Drive.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Jerry Rogers Creek Culvert Replacement contract award

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.36M contract for Central Valley Greenway culvert and paving work
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a $1,360,391 contract to Drake Excavating (2016) Ltd. to replace 16 culverts (underground drainage pipes) and repave the Central Valley Greenway trail between Sperling Avenue and Douglas Road. The project is already budgeted in the City's 2026–2030 Financial Plan, with funding split between City reserves and TransLink.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 14, 2026 council decision if your business operates near the Central Valley Greenway trail.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Central Valley Greenway culvert contract award

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $450,000 contract extension for Rogers cellular services
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change worth $450,000 (including taxes) to keep Rogers Communications Canada Inc. supplying cellular voice and data services to the City on a month-to-month basis. This short-term extension would bridge the gap while the City finishes negotiating a new contract with a different provider found through a public bidding process.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — this is an internal City contract with no direct impact on most businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Rogers cellular contract change approval

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $579,600 contract change for City building HVAC maintenance
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a contract change worth $579,600 (including taxes) with Just Mechanical Ltd. to keep heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems running in City-owned buildings. This is a short-term extension to avoid any gap in service while the City runs a new competitive bidding process to find a long-term provider.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — this affects HVAC service contractors bidding on City work.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — HVAC contract change approval meeting
Just Mechanical Ltd. interim HVAC contract renewal end date

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby's $394,942 Cisco WebEx Contact Center license renewal
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a three-year software subscription with Long View Systems Corporation for approximately $394,942 (taxes included). The subscription keeps the City's phone-based customer service system running — the one residents call for help — and also supports IT staff who provide phone support inside City Hall.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No direct action needed — this is an internal City IT contract with no impact on business owners.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.67M fire protection services contract renewal with A.W Fireguard
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a contract with A.W Fireguard Supplies (1991) Ltd. to inspect, maintain, and repair fire protection and emergency lighting systems at City buildings. The proposed three-year renewal would run from July 1, 2026 to June 30, 2029, at an estimated cost of $1,666,061 including taxes.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you supply fire protection services, watch this decision — it signals how Burnaby procures these contracts.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on hiring an external reviewer to assess Burnaby's financial practices
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on hiring an outside financial expert to review how the City plans its budget, manages day-to-day spending, and prioritizes major investments. The review is meant to help protect the City's financial health and reduce the risk of large tax increases or rising service costs for businesses and residents.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 14, 2026 council decision — it could affect future tax rates for your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — external financial review approval

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 20-Storey Rental Apartment at 5035 Beresford Street Up for Approval
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a rezoning application that would allow a 20-storey rental apartment building with 215 units to be built at 5035 Beresford Street in the Royal Oak area. The site currently holds a printing shop, and the proposal would change its zoning from industrial to a residential development district — meaning the land's permitted use would shift from industrial to multi-family housing.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the Council meeting for the rezoning bylaw's First and Second Reading date if this affects your property or business nearby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ #25-11 rezoning bylaw First and Second Reading — Council meeting

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider: Progress Update on Burnaby's Economic Growth Task Force
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider an update on the Mayor's Task Force on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience, a group of business and community leaders working to build a stronger local economy. The Task Force has held five workshops so far and is now gathering feedback from the business community before presenting final recommendations to Council on June 23, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Task Force's final recommendations to Council on June 23, 2026 — they may affect local business support programs.

key_dates.label: Mayor's Task Force final recommendations presented to Burnaby Council

agenda_briefs.title: Council to Consider: Developer Forfeits $1.36M Instead of Building Fraser River Pier
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report on Oxford Properties' decision to give up $1,363,500 in security — money held as a guarantee — rather than build a planned public pier on the Fraser River at Riverbend Business Park. The developer could not get the required provincial land approval to build on the river, so under a legal agreement registered on the property, they are allowed to forfeit the funds instead. Council will consider how to use that money for a future public amenity.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for Council's future decision on how the $1.36M will be spent — it may fund nearby public amenities.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal for 2026–2031
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would renew the Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) for five years, from April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2031. If approved, the bylaw would provide the Burnaby North Road Business Association with up to $1,755,147.81 in total funding over that period — paid out annually — and recover those costs through a special property tax (called a 'local service tax') charged to eligible business and light-industrial property owners within the BIA boundaries.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property falls within the Burnaby North Road BIA — you may owe a new annual tax if it does.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA bylaw — proposed start date takes effect
Burnaby North Road BIA — annual budget submission deadline (Association)
